content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

January 13, 1977

77-01-214

Mr. Gerald A. Sperry, City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
Stockton, CA 95202

Dear Mr. Sperry:

Thank you for your letter of December 22, 1976, concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. This letter will confirm our conversation of January 3, 1977, in which I advised you that the staff would be unable to provide you with definitive answers to your questions at this time.

As I indicated, the staff can only provide advice to persons requesting guidance on their own behalf or on behalf of half of their clients. Accordingly, if Mr. Coy wishes to seek advice on the matters discussed in your letter, he should authorize you to represent him and we then will be happy to try to assist you. If you do seek future advice, please include additional information with respect to the manner of financing for each entity, the types of decisions which are made by each entity, the interrelationship between the entities, and any additional information which you believe would be helpful.

There is one question posed in your letter which I can answer at the present time. You asked whether Mr. Coy could be prevented from holding office by virtue of potential conflicts of interest. Under the Political Reform Act, a public official is prohibited from making, participating in the making, or using his official position to influence the making of a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87100. However, no provision of the Act, and no provision of a Conflict of Interest Code promulgated pursuant to the Act, prevents a person from assuming office.

Enclosed please find a copy of an opinion request submitted by Charles F. Leach, No. 76-092, which poses questions

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-6001 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6144

Mr. Gerald A. Sperry
Page Two
January 13, 1977


similar to the ones set forth in your letter. I have included
your name on our mailing list for this opinion so that you will
receive the proposed draft opinion when it is ready and will be
able to submit written comments on the draft and/or testify
during the Commission's deliberations with respect to the draft.
If you have any further questions, please contact either me or
Ed Kerry.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

TP:jo

Enclosure

FPPC

DEC 27 9 20 AM '16

CITY OF STOCKTON

OFFICE
OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
STOCKTON, CA 95202

(209) 944-8333

December 22, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento CA 95814

REQUEST FOR ADVISORY OPINION

The Honorable Manuel Silveria, City Councilman for the City of
Stockton, has submitted the following question and hereby requests an advisory opinion from the Fair Political Practices
Commission.

"Is there any conflict of interest in Mr. Ed Coy's
holding the position of membership in the City's
Planning Commission while concurrently holding
positions in the private sector as Executive
Director of the Downtown Stockton Associates;
Executive Director of the Central Parking District;
and Executive Director of the Assessment District
(Business Improvement Area I-B)?"

FACTS

DOWNTOWN STOCKTON ASSOCIATES

The Downtown Stockton Associates is a non-profit corporation
which has for its corporate purposes,

"to secure for its members the benefit of united
effort for the extension and promotion of trade
and commerce; to promote good fellowship and
cooperative relationship among its members; to
secure protection for its members against fraud
and imposition, and to promote in general the
business interest in Stockton, California, and
vicinity." (Article II, By Laws of the Downtown
Stockton Associates).


'Stockton....Someplace Special!'

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 22, 1976
Page 2


Ed Coy receives a salary as Executive Director and serves at
the pleasure of the Board of Directors.

CENTRAL PARKING DISTRICT

The City of Stockton entered into a contract (still in effect)
with the Downtown Stockton Associates under which contractor
(Downtown Stockton Associates) will render certain technical,
professional and managerial services in connection with the
administration of affairs of the Central Parking District.
Ed Coy has been assigned the responsibility by the contractor
(DSA) to run and manage the Central Parking District.  Ed Coy
is paid for his management services by the Downtown Stockton
Associates and not by the Central Parking District.

BUSINESS IMPROVEMENT AREA I-B

Business Improvement Area I-B is a non-profit corporation which
has among its corporate purposes:

"specifically and primarily to advising and making
recommendations to the Council of the City of
Stockton, a municipal corporation of the State of
California, on how to stimulate and support the
general promotion of business and retail trade
activities in the 'Area', the promotion of public
events which are to take place on or in public places
in the 'Area', the decoration of any public place in
the 'Area', the furnishing of music in any public
place in the 'Area'." (Article II, Articles of
Incorporation, Business Improvement Area I-B).

"... No substantial part of its activities shall be
devoted to carrying on propaganda, or otherwise
attempting to influence legislation ...." (Article III,
Articles of Incorporation, Business Improvement
Area I-B).

Ed Coy serves as Executive Director of this Assessment District
having been  elected by the Board of Directors.

Mr. Ed Coy asserts that he, as a member of the Planning Commission
has disqualified himself from voting on issues directly or in-
directly affecting the Downtown District which he represents by
virtue of his position as Executive Director of the Downtown
Stockton Associates.

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 22, 1976
Page 3


Will you please review this matter and advise whether Mr. Ed Coy
is disqualified from serving on the Stockton City Planning
Commission.

If additional facts or information is needed, please advise.


[signature]
GERALD A. SPERRY
CITY ATTORNEY

GAS:cj

cc: City Council
    Planning Commission
    City Manager

sections.question_synthetic: Does Ed Coy have a conflict of interest that would disqualify him from serving on the Stockton City Planning Commission while simultaneously serving as Executive Director of three entities with potential relationships to the City?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide definitive answers without authorization from Mr. Coy and additional information about financing, decision-making authority, and interrelationships between entities. However, the Political Reform Act does not prevent someone from holding office based on potential conflicts of interest; it only prohibits officials from making decisions in which they have financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (December 22, 1976) and FPPC response letter (January 13, 1977). Extracted primarily from the FPPC response as the main document. The response is partial/limited advice indicating the staff needs additional authorization and information to provide full guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is there any conflict of interest in Mr. Ed Coy's holding the position of membership in the City's Planning Commission while concurrently holding positions in the private sector as Executive Director of the Downtown Stockton Associates; Executive Director of the Central Parking District; and Executive Director of the Assessment District (Business Improvement Area I-B)?

CONCLUSION: Under the Political Reform Act, a public official is prohibited from making, participating in the making, or using his official position to influence the making of a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest (Government Code Section 87100). However, no provision of the Act, and no provision of a Conflict of Interest Code promulgated pursuant to the Act, prevents a person from assuming office.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a City Attorney's inquiry regarding whether Ed Coy has a conflict of interest preventing him from serving on the Planning Commission while holding executive positions in three private/quasi-public entities. The FPPC declines to provide definitive advice without Coy's authorization but clarifies that the Political Reform Act does not bar someone from assuming office, only from participating in decisions where they have financial interests.
